content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Ask most people why they still check their signals by hand, and you’ll get the same answer: automating it sounds like a whole engineering project.
Queues, workers, a database, a cloud console with forty tabs. So the signal stays a browser bookmark you open when you remember to.
And a signal you have to remember to check is a signal you’ll forget.
Fortunately, there’s a better way. A short Python file and a scheduler that runs it once a day.
No framework, no queue, no classes.
By the end of this you’ll have a next-day-movers slate landing in your inbox every morning before the close, pulled from one Alphanume endpoint.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Let’s build it.
What next-day-movers actually is
next-day-movers is a daily, model-ranked set of equities most likely to make a large move the next session.
The model scores a liquid, optionable universe on implied vol, realized vol, and vol-structure features, then hands back the names sitting at the top. It regenerates every day at 3:30 PM ET, and every row is point-in-time, so what you pull is what the model actually flagged that afternoon.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

It’s a short slate. About five names per date, not a screen of fifty.
You hit it like any other Alphanume endpoint:
requests.get("https://api.alphanume.com/v1/next-day-movers?api_key=alp_...")Same envelope as everything else on the platform, a count and a data list of rows:
{"count": 5, "data": [ ... ]}Each row carries four fields: date, ticker, return, and absolute_move.
The free tier gives you a rolling 30-day window of delayed data, which is everything you need to build and test this. Today’s live row is reserved for Pro.
One wrinkle to know before the code makes sense. On the newest date, return and absolute_move come back null until that next session closes.
The freshest slate is the names with their moves still pending, which is exactly the list you want emailed to yourself before close.
A few names it actually called
Before wiring anything up, it’s fair to ask whether the list is worth automating. A few of the names it flagged recently, all reproducible on a free key:
date ticker next-session move
2026-06-30 SOC +42.66%
2026-06-26 SLS +24.71%
2026-06-26 FCEL +23.95%
2026-06-25 FCEL +21.98%
2026-06-16 RXT +21.37%Cherry-picking the winners is easy and dishonest, so here’s a full slate. On 2026–06–30 the model flagged five names, and every one of them moved:
ticker next-session move
SOC +42.66%
FCEL -11.44%
SLS -10.21%
AXTI -9.85%
OUST -4.04%The biggest was +42.66%, the smallest -4.04%.
Across the full history, about 49% of flagged names moved more than 5% the next session, about 21% moved more than 10%, and the median absolute move was around 4.8%.
So treat it as a volatility screen. Plenty of names move a little, a meaningful share move a lot, but the model doesn’t call direction for you.
The script
The logic is short and flat. Pull the endpoint, keep the latest date, sort that day’s slate, format it into text.
import requests
import pandas as pdresp = requests.get(
"https://api.alphanume.com/v1/next-day-movers",
params={"api_key": "alp_..."},
)
resp.raise_for_status()
df = pd.json_normalize(resp.json()["data"])The raise_for_status() line is the one beginners skip and later regret. If the key is wrong or the endpoint is down at 6 AM, you find out right there instead of getting a silent, empty email.
Now keep the newest slate and sort it by size of expected move:
latest = df["date"].max()
slate = df[df["date"] == latest].sort_values("absolute_move", ascending=False)Then format the names into a plain-text table. The move column reads pending on the newest date, since return is still null until that session closes:
rows = [f"Movers for {latest}", ""]
for _, r in slate.iterrows():
move = "pending" if pd.isna(r["return"]) else f"{r['return']:+.2f}%"
rows.append(f"{r['ticker']:<6} {move}")body = "\n".join(rows)
print(body)Run it against a free key today and you get the live slate for the upcoming session, moves still pending:
Movers for 2026-07-08AXTI pending
BE pending
FCEL pending
SLS pending
SOC pendingThat print is your test harness. Point latest at an older date instead and the same code fills in the realized moves, so you can eyeball the feed for a week before you trust it with your morning.
The only change between the version that prints and the version that emails is the last step: send body instead of printing it.
Zoomed out, the whole thing is five steps and one file (diagram below): a scheduler fires, the script pulls the endpoint, formats the slate, sends it over SMTP, and it lands in your inbox.
Sending it to yourself
Email out of Python is the standard library plus a login. Gmail is the path of least resistance.
import smtplib
from email.message import EmailMessagemsg = EmailMessage()
msg["Subject"] = f"Movers for {latest}"
msg["From"] = "you@gmail.com"
msg["To"] = "you@gmail.com"
msg.set_content(body)with smtplib.SMTP_SSL("smtp.gmail.com", 465) as server:
server.login("you@gmail.com", "your_app_password")
server.send_message(msg)The one thing that trips everyone up: your_app_password is not your Gmail login password. Google blocks that.
You generate a 16-character App Password at myaccount.google.com/apppasswords, which requires 2-Step Verification turned on first. Paste that into the script and leave your real password out of it.
Prefer Outlook or Zoho? Both work the same way, with an app password, over a slightly different connection.
Outlook is smtp-mail.outlook.com on port 587, Zoho is smtp.zoho.com on port 587, and both use STARTTLS instead of SSL. The only code change is swapping the SSL connection for a plain one that upgrades:
with smtplib.SMTP("smtp-mail.outlook.com", 587) as server:
server.starttls()
server.login("you@outlook.com", "your_app_password")
server.send_message(msg)That’s the entire production script. Pull, rank, format, send.
Putting it on a server
The script works on your machine, which means it works when your machine is on and you remember to run it. The point was to stop remembering.
For that you want it on a box that’s always awake. We use PythonAnywhere, on the paid Developer tier at $10/month, and the setup is three steps:

• Upload the one production file and paste in your API key and app password.

• Create a Scheduled Task that runs the file once a day.

• Pick the time carefully. PythonAnywhere schedules in UTC, so as of the time of writing, 15 minutes before the close will be 19:45.
That’s it. The task fires every evening, and the email is waiting when you sit down.
Final Thoughts
None of this is specific to next-day-movers.
Swap the endpoint and the same pull-rank-format-send skeleton mails you an IV-rank screen, an earnings vol calendar, or a momentum basket instead. The Alphanume envelope is always {"count": N, "data": [...]}, so the code barely changes when the question does.
The real upgrade is smaller than the model or the email. It’s moving a signal out of a browser tab you have to remember to open and into a place that reaches you on its own.
Grab a free key at alphanume.com, get the print version working against last week’s slate, and once you trust it, give it a server and let it run.
The full code for this experiment can be found via the Alphanume Strategy Lab on GitHub.
As always, thanks for reading, and we’ll see you in the next one. 🫡
